content.verdict: Craft is a hosted notes and docs app for writing, planning, and AI-assisted content creation across Docs, Tasks, Calendar, and Whiteboards, built for individuals and small teams who want a polished, synced writing environment. Linkding is a free, self-hosted bookmark manager built to run in a single Docker container, focused narrowly on saving, tagging, and archiving links rather than authoring content. They overlap only at the edges — Craft is for creating documents, Linkding is for collecting and retrieving URLs you've already found.
content.bestForToolA: Craft fits individuals and small teams who want AI-assisted writing (Core/Fast/Max models), whiteboards, and templates in a managed, cross-device-synced app — its Free plan includes 1,500 blocks, 1GB storage, and 15 AI credits.
content.bestForToolB: Linkding fits developers and self-hosters who want a completely free, minimal bookmark archive with automatic metadata fetching, website archiving, and a REST API, deployed in one Docker container with SQLite.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A researcher could save and archive source links in Linkding (using its automatic metadata fetching and website archiving) and then write the actual analysis, summary, or report from those sources as a document in Craft.

keyDifferences.title: Hosting Model
keyDifferences.toolA: Craft is a fully hosted, cross-device-synced SaaS app with no self-hosting option documented.
keyDifferences.toolB: Linkding requires self-hosting, deployed as a single Docker container using SQLite, with no official managed cloud version.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Self-hosting shifts operational responsibility (backups, updates, uptime) onto the user in exchange for data control and zero subscription cost.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Privacy-conscious self-hosters and homelab users benefit from Linkding's model, while non-technical users benefit from Craft's fully managed sync.

keyDifferences.title: Pricing Structure
keyDifferences.toolA: Craft's Free plan caps out at 1,500 blocks, 1GB storage, and 15 AI credits; Plus is €4.8/month billed monthly (€8.0/month billed yearly), Family is €9.0/month, and Team is €50.0/month.
keyDifferences.toolB: Linkding has no pricing plans documented at all — it is free, open-source software with no paid tier.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Craft's usage caps and AI credit limits mean cost scales with content volume and AI use, while Linkding has no billing to plan around beyond hosting infrastructure.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Budget-conscious technical users benefit from Linkding's zero-cost model, while users wanting a managed product with support benefit from Craft's paid tiers.

keyDifferences.title: Core Purpose
keyDifferences.toolA: Craft centers on content creation: Docs, Tasks embedded in documents, a built-in Calendar, Whiteboards for visual collaboration, and Publish & Share for direct publishing.
keyDifferences.toolB: Linkding centers on content retrieval: automatic metadata fetching (title, description, icon, preview image), bulk editing, and Netscape HTML import/export for bookmark libraries.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Choosing the wrong category tool means either drafting documents in a bookmark manager or trying to archive links inside a notes app — neither works well.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Writers and planners benefit from Craft; people managing large personal or team link libraries benefit from Linkding.

keyDifferences.title: AI Writing Features
keyDifferences.toolA: Craft includes "Write with AI" with access to Core, Fast, and Max AI models on paid plans, plus 15 AI credits on the Free plan and 50 monthly AI credits on Plus.
keyDifferences.toolB: Linkding does not document any AI writing or summarization features.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: AI-assisted drafting and editing is a differentiator for content creation but irrelevant to a tool whose job is just storing links.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Writers who want AI help drafting content benefit from Craft; this dimension doesn't apply to Linkding's use case.

keyDifferences.title: API and Extensibility
keyDifferences.toolA: Craft integrates with external AI tools — Claude, ChatGPT, Cursor, VS Code, and Apple Shortcuts — but does not document its own REST API.
keyDifferences.toolB: Linkding ships official browser extensions for Firefox and Chrome plus a REST API for building custom scripts and third-party integrations.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A documented REST API lets teams automate saving, tagging, or syncing bookmarks programmatically, which matters for building custom workflows.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Developers building automation around saved links benefit from Linkding's REST API; users who live inside other AI tools benefit from Craft's integrations.

faqs.question: Is Craft or Linkding free to use?
faqs.answer: Both have free options, but they work differently. Craft's Free plan is capped at 1,500 blocks of content, 1GB storage, and 15 AI credits, with paid Plus/Family/Team tiers for more. Linkding is entirely free and open source with no pricing plans at all, though you must provide your own hosting.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Craft the way I self-host Linkding?
faqs.answer: No self-hosting option is documented for Craft — it's a hosted SaaS app. Linkding, by contrast, requires self-hosting and is designed to run in a single Docker container with SQLite.

faqs.question: Does either tool have AI features?
faqs.answer: Craft has AI features documented, including a "Write with AI" tool with Core, Fast, and Max models and monthly AI credits. Linkding does not document any AI functionality.

faqs.question: Which tool has an API for automation?
faqs.answer: Linkding documents a REST API for building custom scripts and third-party integrations. Craft does not document a public REST API, though it integrates with external AI tools like Claude, ChatGPT, and Cursor.

faqs.question: Can I use Craft and Linkding together?
faqs.answer: Yes — they serve different jobs. You could archive and tag research links in Linkding, then write up findings or reports as documents in Craft.

faqs.question: Which is better for a small team?
faqs.answer: Craft offers dedicated Family (2-6 Plus accounts) and Team (up to 10 accounts) plans with Shared Space collaboration for €9/month and €50/month respectively. Linkding supports multiple user accounts with bookmark sharing built into the free, self-hosted app.

content.verdict: Linkding is a narrow, self-hosted bookmark manager built to run in a single Docker container and do one job well: save, tag, and archive links. Notion is a broad, AI-powered workspace combining docs, wikis, project management, and databases that teams use to run their company. Comparing them head-to-head only makes sense if you're weighing 'a dedicated free bookmark tool' against 'a general workspace that could also hold bookmarks as a database.'
content.bestForToolA: Linkding fits self-hosters who want a free, minimal, single-purpose bookmark archive with automatic metadata fetching, website archiving, and a REST API, without paying for or configuring a broader workspace.
content.bestForToolB: Notion fits individuals or teams who need one connected workspace for docs, wikis, task tracking, and databases with flexible views (table, board, calendar, timeline) and built-in AI writing and Q&A.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A team could use Linkding as a lightweight, self-hosted link archive for research and reference material while running actual project docs, task tracking, and internal wikis in Notion — Linkding's REST API could even feed saved links into a Notion database.

keyDifferences.title: Scope of the Product
keyDifferences.toolA: Linkding does one thing: bookmark storage, tagging, and archiving, with features like automatic metadata fetching and website archiving via local snapshots or the Internet Archive.
keyDifferences.toolB: Notion combines block-based pages, flexible databases, docs, wikis, and project management into a single connected workspace.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A single-purpose tool is simpler to adopt and operate but can't replace a full workspace, while an all-in-one tool requires more setup investment to structure well.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams that already have a project/docs tool and just need bookmarks benefit from Linkding's focus; teams starting from scratch benefit from Notion's breadth.

keyDifferences.title: Pricing
keyDifferences.toolA: Linkding has no pricing plans documented — it is free, open-source software you self-host.
keyDifferences.toolB: Notion is freemium: a Free plan, then Plus at $10 per member/month, Business at $20 per member/month, and custom Enterprise pricing.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Per-seat SaaS pricing scales cost with team size, while a free self-hosted tool has no per-seat cost but requires hosting.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Cost-sensitive solo users benefit from Linkding's free model; growing teams needing collaboration features accept Notion's per-seat pricing for the added functionality.

keyDifferences.title: AI Features
keyDifferences.toolA: Linkding does not document any AI features.
keyDifferences.toolB: Notion includes AI-assisted writing, Q&A over a team's own Notion content, and configurable AI Custom Agents that automate specific workspace workflows.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: AI-assisted writing and Q&A can meaningfully speed up documentation and research synthesis work inside a workspace, which a bookmark manager isn't built to do.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams wanting AI-assisted content creation and search benefit from Notion; this dimension is not relevant to Linkding's use case.

keyDifferences.title: Hosting and Data Control
keyDifferences.toolA: Linkding requires self-hosting (single Docker container with SQLite) with no official managed cloud version; users are responsible for their own backups and updates.
keyDifferences.toolB: Notion is a fully hosted SaaS product with no self-hosting option documented.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Self-hosting gives full data control but adds operational responsibility, while a managed SaaS removes that burden but ties data to the vendor's infrastructure.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Privacy- or compliance-conscious self-hosters benefit from Linkding; teams that don't want to run infrastructure benefit from Notion.

keyDifferences.title: Collaboration Depth
keyDifferences.toolA: Linkding supports multiple user accounts with bookmark sharing between users, but has no native mobile apps and relies on browser extensions and the web UI.
keyDifferences.toolB: Notion supports team-wide collaboration across docs, databases, and project views, with Business and Enterprise tiers adding admin controls and advanced collaboration features.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Real-time, structured team collaboration across many content types requires more built-in tooling than a bookmark manager offers.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Cross-functional teams benefit from Notion's collaboration depth; small groups just sharing links benefit from Linkding's simpler multi-user model.

faqs.question: Is Linkding a replacement for Notion?
faqs.answer: No — Linkding only manages bookmarks (saving, tagging, archiving). Notion is a broad workspace covering docs, wikis, task tracking, and databases. They're not interchangeable.

faqs.question: Which one is free?
faqs.answer: Linkding is entirely free and open source with no pricing plans. Notion has a Free plan but paid tiers start at $10 per member/month for Plus and $20 per member/month for Business.

faqs.question: Does Linkding have AI features like Notion?
faqs.answer: No AI features are documented for Linkding. Notion includes AI-assisted writing, Q&A over your workspace content, and configurable AI Custom Agents.

faqs.question: Can I self-host either tool?
faqs.answer: Linkding requires self-hosting via a single Docker container with SQLite. Notion does not document a self-hosting option — it's a hosted SaaS product only.

faqs.question: Can I use both together?
faqs.answer: Yes. You could keep a dedicated, self-hosted bookmark archive in Linkding for research links while running project docs, wikis, and team collaboration in Notion, potentially syncing saved links into a Notion database via Linkding's REST API.

faqs.question: Which has native mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Linkding does not document native mobile apps and relies on browser extensions and its web UI. Notion's mobile app support is not detailed in the available facts, but its broader platform is built for cross-device team use.

content.verdict: LibreOffice is a free, open-source desktop office suite — Writer, Calc, Impress, Draw, Base, and Math — built for creating traditional documents, spreadsheets, and presentations, largely offline. Notion is a freemium, cloud-hosted, AI-powered workspace built around block-based pages and flexible databases for teams to run docs, wikis, and project tracking together. One replaces Microsoft Office; the other replaces a stack of collaboration tools.
content.bestForToolA: LibreOffice fits individuals and organizations that need traditional office file types (documents, spreadsheets, presentations, diagrams, formulas) at zero cost, under the Mozilla Public License v2.0, without depending on a cloud service.
content.bestForToolB: Notion fits teams that want a single connected workspace for docs, wikis, databases, and project management, with AI-assisted writing, Q&A over their own content, and configurable AI Custom Agents, starting free and scaling to $10-$20 per member/month.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A small organization might draft formal reports, spreadsheets, or presentations for external distribution in LibreOffice (leveraging its Writer, Calc, and Impress apps) while running internal project tracking, wikis, and team collaboration in Notion.

keyDifferences.title: Deployment Model
keyDifferences.toolA: LibreOffice is installed desktop software; it works offline and has no cloud service or pricing plans documented.
keyDifferences.toolB: Notion is a cloud-hosted SaaS workspace with no self-hosting or offline-first option documented.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Offline desktop software works without internet access and keeps files locally, while cloud SaaS enables real-time multi-device collaboration but requires connectivity.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Users without reliable internet or with strict local-data requirements benefit from LibreOffice; distributed teams needing real-time collaboration benefit from Notion.

keyDifferences.title: Pricing
keyDifferences.toolA: LibreOffice is completely free with no pricing plans documented and no official paid support tier listed.
keyDifferences.toolB: Notion is freemium: Free, then Plus at $10 per member/month, Business at $20 per member/month, and custom Enterprise pricing.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Zero-cost software has no budget impact regardless of team size, while per-seat SaaS pricing grows with headcount.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Budget-constrained individuals and organizations benefit from LibreOffice; teams that value ongoing cloud features and support may accept Notion's per-seat cost.

keyDifferences.title: Application Scope
keyDifferences.toolA: LibreOffice bundles six distinct applications — Writer (word processing), Calc (spreadsheets), Impress (presentations), Draw (diagramming), Base (databases), and Math (formula editing).
keyDifferences.toolB: Notion is a single flexible system of block-based pages and databases that can be viewed as a table, board, calendar, or timeline, rather than separate dedicated apps.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Dedicated applications match familiar file formats and workflows, while a unified block/database system trades format familiarity for flexibility and interconnection.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Users needing traditional file formats (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx-style workflows) benefit from LibreOffice; users wanting one interconnected system benefit from Notion.

keyDifferences.title: AI Features
keyDifferences.toolA: LibreOffice does not document any AI writing, summarization, or automation features.
keyDifferences.toolB: Notion includes AI-assisted writing, Q&A over a team's own Notion content, and configurable AI Custom Agents for workspace automation.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: AI-assisted drafting and search can meaningfully speed up content and knowledge work, a capability entirely absent from a traditional offline office suite.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams wanting AI-assisted content creation benefit from Notion; this isn't a factor for LibreOffice users.

keyDifferences.title: Real-Time Collaboration
keyDifferences.toolA: LibreOffice does not document built-in real-time multi-user editing across its applications.
keyDifferences.toolB: Notion is built for real-time collaboration across pages and databases as a core part of the product.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Teams co-editing the same content simultaneously need a tool designed for concurrent, synced editing.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Collaborative teams benefit from Notion; individuals producing standalone documents benefit from LibreOffice's simpler, file-based model.

faqs.question: Is LibreOffice really free, unlike Notion's paid tiers?
faqs.answer: Yes. LibreOffice has no pricing plans documented — it's free, open-source software under the Mozilla Public License v2.0. Notion has a Free plan too, but paid tiers (Plus, Business, Enterprise) start at $10 per member/month for added collaboration and admin features.

faqs.question: Can I use LibreOffice for team collaboration like Notion?
faqs.answer: LibreOffice does not document built-in real-time multi-user editing. Notion is built specifically for real-time collaboration across pages and databases, making it better suited to teams working together on the same content simultaneously.

faqs.question: Does LibreOffice have AI features like Notion?
faqs.answer: No AI features are documented for LibreOffice. Notion includes AI-assisted writing, Q&A over your workspace content, and configurable AI Custom Agents.

faqs.question: Which applications does LibreOffice include?
faqs.answer: LibreOffice bundles Writer (word processing), Calc (spreadsheets), Impress (presentations), Draw (diagramming), Base (databases), and Math (formula editing) into one suite.

faqs.question: Can LibreOffice and Notion be used together?
faqs.answer: Yes. Many organizations produce formal documents, spreadsheets, or presentations in LibreOffice while running project tracking, internal wikis, and team databases in Notion.

faqs.question: Does either tool require an internet connection?
faqs.answer: LibreOffice is installed desktop software that works offline. Notion is a cloud-hosted SaaS workspace with no offline-first or self-hosting option documented.

content.verdict: Notion is a broad, AI-powered workspace for docs, wikis, project management, and databases that teams use to run their company. wallabag is a narrow, self-hostable read-it-later app built specifically to archive web pages for offline reading. They rarely compete directly — Notion organizes and creates work, wallabag saves and preserves articles you find elsewhere.
content.bestForToolA: Notion fits teams needing one connected workspace with flexible database views, AI-assisted writing, and AI Q&A over their own content, priced from a Free plan up to $20 per member/month for Business.
content.bestForToolB: wallabag fits readers who want a self-hostable (or third-party hosted) archive of saved articles with offline reading, RSS reader and e-reader support, and dedicated migration tools from Pocket and Omnivore.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A researcher could save and read long-form articles offline in wallabag, then bring key excerpts and analysis into a Notion database or wiki page as part of a larger knowledge-management workspace.

keyDifferences.title: Core Purpose
keyDifferences.toolA: Notion combines block-based pages, flexible database views, docs, wikis, and project management for teams running work end-to-end.
keyDifferences.toolB: wallabag exists to archive web pages for later, offline reading, with multi-platform apps, browser extensions, RSS reader, and e-reader support.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A read-it-later tool and a company workspace solve fundamentally different problems, so picking between them depends on whether you're organizing team work or archiving articles to read.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams running projects and documentation benefit from Notion; individuals building a personal reading archive benefit from wallabag.

keyDifferences.title: Hosting Requirement
keyDifferences.toolA: Notion is a fully hosted SaaS workspace with no self-hosting option documented.
keyDifferences.toolB: wallabag requires self-hosting or a third-party host to use, unlike a fully managed read-it-later service.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Self-hosting gives control over data and cost but adds operational responsibility that a managed SaaS removes.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams that don't want to manage infrastructure benefit from Notion; privacy-focused individuals comfortable self-hosting benefit from wallabag.

keyDifferences.title: Migration Tooling
keyDifferences.toolA: Notion's documented facts do not mention dedicated migration tools from other read-it-later or bookmarking services.
keyDifferences.toolB: wallabag provides dedicated migration guides for moving an existing library from both Pocket and Omnivore, not just a generic import.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Purpose-built migration tools reduce the friction of switching services and preserve reading history and tags.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Users switching away from Pocket or Omnivore benefit specifically from wallabag's migration guides.

keyDifferences.title: AI Features
keyDifferences.toolA: Notion includes AI-assisted writing, Q&A over a team's own Notion content, and configurable AI Custom Agents.
keyDifferences.toolB: wallabag does not document any AI features — its focus is archiving and offline reading.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: AI-assisted writing and search are relevant to content creation and knowledge work, not to a tool whose job is preserving articles as-is.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams wanting AI-assisted drafting and search benefit from Notion; this dimension doesn't apply to wallabag's use case.

keyDifferences.title: Reading Platform Support
keyDifferences.toolA: Notion's facts do not document RSS reader or e-reader integration.
keyDifferences.toolB: wallabag documents apps, browser extensions, RSS reader support, and e-reader compatibility for offline reading across devices.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Dedicated e-reader and RSS support matters specifically for people who read saved long-form content away from a screen with notifications.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Heavy readers who use e-readers or RSS feeds benefit from wallabag's platform coverage.

faqs.question: Are Notion and wallabag direct competitors?
faqs.answer: Not really. Notion is a broad workspace for docs, wikis, and project management, while wallabag is a focused read-it-later app for archiving web pages. They serve different jobs.

faqs.question: Is wallabag free like Notion's free plan?
faqs.answer: wallabag is freemium and requires self-hosting or a third-party host to use — there's no fully managed free tier. Notion offers a hosted Free plan directly, with paid Plus ($10/member/month) and Business ($20/member/month) tiers.

faqs.question: Can I migrate my saved articles into wallabag?
faqs.answer: Yes — wallabag provides dedicated migration guides for moving an existing library from Pocket or Omnivore, not just a generic import tool.

faqs.question: Does wallabag have AI features like Notion?
faqs.answer: No AI features are documented for wallabag. Notion includes AI-assisted writing, Q&A over your own content, and configurable AI Custom Agents.

faqs.question: Can I read wallabag articles on an e-reader?
faqs.answer: Yes, wallabag documents e-reader and RSS reader support for offline reading across devices, which is not documented for Notion.

faqs.question: Should I use both tools?
faqs.answer: It can make sense: archive and read long-form articles offline in wallabag, then summarize or reference key material inside a Notion workspace alongside your other docs and project work.

content.verdict: EtherCalc is a free, open-source, self-hostable spreadsheet built for real-time collaborative editing, shared simply by sending a URL. Notion is a much broader, freemium AI workspace where databases are one of many connected content types alongside docs, wikis, and project management. Choose based on whether you need a dedicated spreadsheet or a full connected workspace with database views built in.
content.bestForToolA: EtherCalc fits technical users who want a free, self-hostable, real-time collaborative spreadsheet with a REST API and XLSX import/export, deployable via Docker Compose or Cloudflare Workers.
content.bestForToolB: Notion fits teams that want spreadsheet-like database views (table, board, calendar, timeline) embedded inside a broader connected workspace with AI-assisted writing and Q&A, from a Free plan up to $20 per member/month for Business.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A technical team could run quick, disposable collaborative calculations in a self-hosted EtherCalc instance (sharing just a URL) while keeping their structured, long-lived project data and documentation in Notion's databases and pages.

keyDifferences.title: Core Purpose
keyDifferences.toolA: EtherCalc is a dedicated spreadsheet application focused solely on real-time collaborative editing of tabular, formula-based data.
keyDifferences.toolB: Notion is a general workspace combining block-based pages, flexible databases, docs, and project management, with spreadsheet-like table views as one feature among many.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A single-purpose spreadsheet is simpler and faster to spin up for pure number-crunching, while a general workspace suits teams organizing many types of content together.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Users who just need a shared spreadsheet benefit from EtherCalc's focus; teams wanting spreadsheets connected to docs and tasks benefit from Notion.

keyDifferences.title: Pricing
keyDifferences.toolA: EtherCalc is free and open source with no pricing plans documented.
keyDifferences.toolB: Notion is freemium: Free, then Plus at $10 per member/month, Business at $20 per member/month, and custom Enterprise pricing.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A zero-cost tool removes budget considerations entirely, while per-seat SaaS pricing scales with team size.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Cost-sensitive technical users benefit from EtherCalc; teams that value a broader feature set may accept Notion's per-seat pricing.

keyDifferences.title: Collaboration Model
keyDifferences.toolA: EtherCalc's collaboration model is simple: share the spreadsheet's URL, and anyone with the link can edit it together in real time.
keyDifferences.toolB: Notion's collaboration is workspace-based, with member roles, permissions, and admin controls scaling up through Business and Enterprise tiers.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: URL-based sharing is fast and frictionless for ad hoc collaboration, while workspace-based permissions suit organizations needing access control.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Ad hoc collaborators benefit from EtherCalc's simplicity; organizations needing structured permissions benefit from Notion.

keyDifferences.title: Deployment
keyDifferences.toolA: EtherCalc can be self-hosted via Docker Compose without requiring Redis or other external services, or deployed to Cloudflare Workers as an alternative.
keyDifferences.toolB: Notion is a fully hosted SaaS workspace with no self-hosting option documented.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Self-hosting gives full control over where spreadsheet data lives, which matters for teams with data-residency or privacy requirements.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Technical teams wanting infrastructure control benefit from EtherCalc; teams that prefer a fully managed service benefit from Notion.

keyDifferences.title: AI Features
keyDifferences.toolA: EtherCalc does not document any AI features.
keyDifferences.toolB: Notion includes AI-assisted writing, Q&A over a team's own Notion content, and configurable AI Custom Agents.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: AI-assisted content creation and search are relevant to broader knowledge work, not to a tool focused purely on spreadsheet calculations.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams wanting AI-assisted work benefit from Notion; this dimension doesn't apply to EtherCalc's focused use case.

faqs.question: Is EtherCalc a replacement for Notion's databases?
faqs.answer: Not exactly. EtherCalc is a dedicated, formula-capable spreadsheet, while Notion's database views are structured records that can be displayed as a table, board, calendar, or timeline within a broader workspace — they're not documented as a formula-driven spreadsheet.

faqs.question: Which tool is free?
faqs.answer: EtherCalc is completely free and open source with no pricing plans. Notion has a Free plan but paid tiers start at $10 per member/month for Plus.

faqs.question: Can I self-host EtherCalc or Notion?
faqs.answer: EtherCalc can be self-hosted via Docker Compose or deployed to Cloudflare Workers. Notion does not document a self-hosting option — it's SaaS only.

faqs.question: How do I collaborate on an EtherCalc spreadsheet?
faqs.answer: You share the spreadsheet's URL with others, and they can edit it together in real time — no accounts or permissions system is documented beyond that.

faqs.question: Does EtherCalc have AI features?
faqs.answer: No AI features are documented for EtherCalc. Notion includes AI-assisted writing, Q&A over your workspace content, and configurable AI Custom Agents.

faqs.question: Can EtherCalc and Notion be used together?
faqs.answer: Yes — a team could use a self-hosted EtherCalc instance for quick, shareable calculations while keeping structured, long-term project data and documentation organized in Notion.

content.verdict: Notion is an AI-powered workspace for docs, wikis, project management, and databases that teams use to run their company day to day. Webrecorder is a nonprofit suite of open-source web archiving tools — Browsertrix, ArchiveWeb.page, and ReplayWeb.page — built for capturing and replaying web pages, including login-protected and interactive content, for long-term digital preservation. These tools rarely compete: one organizes team work, the other preserves the web.
content.bestForToolA: Notion fits teams that want a single connected workspace with flexible database views, AI-assisted writing, and AI Q&A over their own content, priced from Free up to $20 per member/month for Business.
content.bestForToolB: Webrecorder fits libraries, universities, governments, and cultural organizations that need to capture and replay complex, interactive, or login-protected web pages for digital preservation, using free tools like ArchiveWeb.page and ReplayWeb.page or the tiered Browsertrix crawling platform.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A research or archival team could use Webrecorder's tools to capture and preserve web-based sources at scale, then organize project plans, findings, and documentation about that archival work inside a Notion workspace.

keyDifferences.title: Core Purpose
keyDifferences.toolA: Notion combines block-based pages, flexible databases, docs, and project management into one workspace for running team operations.
keyDifferences.toolB: Webrecorder builds specialized tools for capturing and replaying web pages — including interactive, social media, and login-protected content — for digital preservation.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Choosing between a productivity workspace and a specialized web-archiving toolkit depends entirely on whether the goal is organizing team work or preserving web content.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams running day-to-day operations benefit from Notion; libraries, universities, and archival institutions benefit from Webrecorder's purpose-built tools.

keyDifferences.title: Pricing Model
keyDifferences.toolA: Notion is freemium with a Free plan, then Plus at $10 per member/month, Business at $20 per member/month, and custom Enterprise pricing.
keyDifferences.toolB: Webrecorder mixes free and paid components: ArchiveWeb.page and ReplayWeb.page are free, while Browsertrix (the automated crawling platform) offers a free trial with paid, tiered plans beyond that.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Understanding which specific components are free versus paid matters for budgeting large-scale archiving projects versus everyday workspace use.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Casual users capturing individual pages benefit from Webrecorder's free tools; organizations needing automated, large-scale crawling budget for Browsertrix; teams needing a workspace budget per-seat for Notion.

keyDifferences.title: Target User Base
keyDifferences.toolA: Notion's documented use case is general team collaboration across docs, wikis, and project management.
keyDifferences.toolB: Webrecorder is run by a nonprofit and documented as used by libraries, universities, governments, and cultural organizations for digital preservation.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Tools built for a specific institutional mission (preservation) often have deeper capabilities for that mission than general-purpose software.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Archival and preservation institutions benefit from Webrecorder's mission-built tools; general business teams benefit from Notion's broad applicability.

keyDifferences.title: Handling Complex Web Content
keyDifferences.toolA: Notion's facts do not document any capability to capture or archive external web pages.
keyDifferences.toolB: Webrecorder's tools can archive pages that require a login, along with interactive and social media content, and use deduplication technology to reduce storage requirements for large archives.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Simple crawlers often miss dynamic, login-gated, or interactive web content, which is exactly what Webrecorder is built to capture.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Anyone needing to preserve complex or gated web content benefits specifically from Webrecorder; this isn't a Notion use case.

keyDifferences.title: Open Source Availability
keyDifferences.toolA: Notion's facts do not document it as open source.
keyDifferences.toolB: Webrecorder provides open-source libraries, command-line tools, and file format specifications on GitHub, alongside its free ArchiveWeb.page and ReplayWeb.page tools.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Open-source availability lets technical teams build custom archiving pipelines and inspect exactly how capture and replay work.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Developers and technical archivists benefit from Webrecorder's open-source tooling.

rows.note: Webrecorder's Browsertrix platform

rows.feature: AI Q&A over your own content

rows.note: Notion Free plan; Webrecorder's ArchiveWeb.page and ReplayWeb.page are free
Notion: $10-$20/member/month; Browsertrix: paid tiers beyond free trial

faqs.question: Are Notion and Webrecorder used for the same purpose?
faqs.answer: No. Notion is a workspace for docs, wikis, and project management. Webrecorder is a suite of tools for capturing and replaying web pages for digital preservation. They serve entirely different needs.

faqs.question: Is Webrecorder free like Notion's free plan?
faqs.answer: Partially. ArchiveWeb.page and ReplayWeb.page are free Webrecorder tools. Browsertrix, its automated crawling platform, offers a free trial but then requires a paid, tiered plan. Notion offers a hosted Free plan, with paid tiers from $10 per member/month.

faqs.question: Can Webrecorder capture pages that require a login?
faqs.answer: Yes — Webrecorder's tools are built to capture login-protected pages and interactive content, including social media posts, which simple crawlers typically miss.

faqs.question: Who uses Webrecorder?
faqs.answer: Webrecorder is used by libraries, universities, governments, and cultural organizations for digital preservation, run by a nonprofit focused on that mission.

faqs.question: Does Notion have web archiving features like Webrecorder?
faqs.answer: No — Notion's documented features center on docs, databases, and AI-assisted writing and Q&A, with no web-page capture or archiving capability documented.

faqs.question: Is Webrecorder open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, its developer tools, libraries, and file format specifications are open source and available on GitHub, in addition to the free ArchiveWeb.page and ReplayWeb.page apps.

content.verdict: Permify is an open-source, Zanzibar-inspired authorization engine that developers embed into applications to enforce fine-grained RBAC, ABAC, and ReBAC access control. Vanta is a compliance automation platform that continuously monitors security controls and streamlines audits across frameworks like SOC 2, ISO 27001, and HIPAA. They sit in adjacent but distinct categories: Permify answers "who can access what inside our application," while Vanta answers "can we prove to auditors that our organization's security controls work."
content.bestForToolA: Permify fits engineering teams building an application that needs a dedicated, self-hostable authorization service supporting RBAC, ABAC, and ReBAC in one schema, with access checks reported as fast as 10ms and either a free AGPL-3.0 Community edition or managed Permify Cloud.
content.bestForToolB: Vanta fits security and compliance teams that need to continuously monitor controls, automate audit evidence collection, and respond to security questionnaires across 20+ frameworks including SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, GDPR, and FedRAMP, backed by 16,000+ customers and 400+ integrations.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A growing SaaS company could use Permify to enforce fine-grained authorization inside its own product while using Vanta to continuously monitor and prove its overall security posture — including access-control practices — to auditors and enterprise customers pursuing SOC 2 or ISO 27001.

keyDifferences.title: Product Category
keyDifferences.toolA: Permify is an authorization engine — infrastructure developers integrate into their own applications to centralize permission logic instead of hardcoding it per app.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vanta is a compliance automation platform for continuously monitoring security controls and preparing for third-party audits.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: These tools solve different problems for different teams and are not interchangeable, despite both being security-adjacent.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Application engineering teams benefit from Permify; security/GRC teams benefit from Vanta.

keyDifferences.title: Pricing Transparency
keyDifferences.toolA: Permify's Community edition is free and self-hosted under AGPL-3.0; Permify Cloud is custom, usage-based pricing.
keyDifferences.toolB: All four Vanta tiers — Essentials, Plus, Professional, and Enterprise — are quote-based with no published pricing, requiring a sales conversation to compare plans.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A free, self-hostable tier lets teams start using Permify at zero cost, while Vanta buyers must engage sales before knowing what any tier costs.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Budget-conscious engineering teams benefit from Permify's free Community edition; enterprises comfortable with a sales-led buying process work within Vanta's model.

keyDifferences.title: Deployment Options
keyDifferences.toolA: Permify can be fully self-hosted (Community, AGPL-3.0) or run as Permify Cloud, a managed offering with SOC2 and GDPR compliance across AWS, GCP, and Azure.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vanta is a SaaS platform with no self-hosting option documented.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Self-hosting matters for teams with strict data-residency or infrastructure-control requirements, which isn't a choice Vanta offers.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams needing infrastructure control benefit from Permify's self-hosted option; teams wanting a fully managed compliance tool benefit from Vanta's SaaS model.

keyDifferences.title: AI Features
keyDifferences.toolA: Permify does not document any AI features.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vanta includes a Vanta AI Agent for drafting policies, completing security questionnaires, and flagging compliance issues, plus AI-powered questionnaire automation (25 questionnaires per year on the Plus plan).
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: AI-assisted questionnaire responses can significantly cut the manual effort compliance teams spend on vendor security reviews.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Security/compliance teams handling frequent questionnaires benefit from Vanta's AI Agent; this isn't a feature category Permify competes in.

keyDifferences.title: Company Backing and Scale
keyDifferences.toolA: Permify was acquired by FusionAuth in November 2025, which committed to keeping it open source, with a unified pricing model planned for 2026.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vanta was founded in 2018, is headquartered in San Francisco, and states it is used by 16,000+ customers with 400+ tool integrations.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Vendor stability and scale of adoption affect long-term risk when choosing infrastructure a business depends on.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Enterprises wanting a proven, large-scale vendor benefit from evaluating Vanta's stated customer base; teams comfortable with a recently-acquired open-source project benefit from Permify's continued open-source commitment.

rows.feature: Compliance framework monitoring (SOC2, ISO27001, HIPAA, etc.)
rows.note: Vanta: 20+ frameworks including FedRAMP and CMMC
Vanta AI Agent
25/year on Plus plan
Permify docs report checks as fast as 10ms
Permify Community, AGPL-3.0, free
Permify Cloud (custom usage-based); Vanta all tiers quote-based
Permify Community is free; both companies' paid tiers are quote-based
Vanta: 400+ integrations

faqs.question: Are Permify and Vanta direct competitors?
faqs.answer: No. Permify is an authorization engine developers embed into applications for access control (RBAC/ABAC/ReBAC). Vanta is a compliance automation platform for monitoring security controls and preparing audits. They address different problems within the broader security space.

faqs.question: Is Permify free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Permify's core Community edition is open source under AGPL-3.0 and can be self-hosted for free. A managed Permify Cloud option with custom, usage-based pricing is also available.

faqs.question: Does Vanta publish its pricing?
faqs.answer: No. All four Vanta plans — Essentials, Plus, Professional, and Enterprise — are quote-based, and comparing them requires contacting sales for a demo.

faqs.question: Which tool has AI features?
faqs.answer: Vanta has a documented Vanta AI Agent for drafting policies, completing questionnaires, and flagging compliance issues, plus AI-powered questionnaire automation. Permify does not document any AI features.

faqs.question: Can Permify and Vanta be used together?
faqs.answer: Yes — a company could use Permify to enforce fine-grained authorization inside its product while using Vanta to continuously monitor and document its broader security and compliance posture for auditors.

faqs.question: Who maintains Permify now?
faqs.answer: Permify was acquired by FusionAuth in November 2025. FusionAuth has stated Permify remains open source and continues to be developed as a standalone authorization engine, with a unified pricing model planned for 2026.

content.verdict: openarchiver is an open-source, self-hosted platform for email backup, compliance, and eDiscovery with full-text search. Permify is an open-source, Zanzibar-inspired authorization engine for building fine-grained RBAC, ABAC, and ReBAC access control into applications. Both are open-source, self-hostable, compliance-adjacent infrastructure tools, but they solve unrelated problems — one archives email communications, the other governs who can access what inside software.
content.bestForToolA: openarchiver fits IT and compliance teams that need to archive email from Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, or generic IMAP servers, or import historical PST/EML files, for backup, eDiscovery, and full-text search.
content.bestForToolB: Permify fits engineering teams building an application that needs a dedicated, self-hostable authorization service supporting RBAC, ABAC, and ReBAC in a single schema, with access checks reported as fast as 10ms.
content.whoNeedsBoth: An organization building an internal compliance platform could use Permify to control which employees or roles can access which archived mailboxes inside their own admin tooling, while using openarchiver as the underlying system that actually archives and indexes the email content.

keyDifferences.title: Core Purpose
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver archives email communications for backup, compliance, and eDiscovery, with full-text search across archived messages.
keyDifferences.toolB: Permify centralizes authorization logic — RBAC, ABAC, and ReBAC — so applications don't need to hardcode permission checks per feature.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Confusing these categories wastes evaluation time; one is a data-archiving system, the other is developer infrastructure for access control.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: IT/compliance teams need openarchiver's archiving; application engineering teams need Permify's authorization logic.

keyDifferences.title: Data Source Integrations
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver connects directly to Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace, plus generic IMAP servers, and can import existing PST and EML files without a live mailbox connection.
keyDifferences.toolB: Permify has no email or mailbox connectors documented — it works via its own schema language and API calls made from an application.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Direct connectors to major email platforms determine how easily an organization can start archiving without custom integration work.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams migrating or backing up existing mail systems benefit from openarchiver's connectors; this isn't a relevant dimension for Permify.

keyDifferences.title: Deployment Options
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver is documented as a self-hosted platform, with no managed cloud version documented.
keyDifferences.toolB: Permify offers both a free, self-hosted Community edition (AGPL-3.0) and a managed Permify Cloud with SOC2 and GDPR compliance across AWS, GCP, and Azure.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Having a managed cloud option removes hosting burden for teams that don't want to run infrastructure themselves.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams wanting a managed option benefit from Permify Cloud; openarchiver users must currently plan to self-host.

keyDifferences.title: Pricing Transparency
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver is tagged as freemium, but no specific pricing plans or tiers are documented.
keyDifferences.toolB: Permify documents two clear tiers: Community (free, self-hosted) and Permify Cloud (custom, usage-based).
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Clearer tiering makes it easier to know what you get for free versus what requires payment before evaluating a tool.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Buyers wanting upfront pricing clarity benefit from Permify's documented tiers over openarchiver's undocumented ones.

keyDifferences.title: Ownership and Backing
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver's founding, company, and headquarters information is not documented in the available facts.
keyDifferences.toolB: Permify was acquired by FusionAuth in November 2025, which has committed to keeping it open source, with a unified pricing model planned for 2026.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Knowing who backs and maintains open-source infrastructure affects confidence in its long-term support.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Risk-averse buyers evaluating vendor stability get more documented signal from Permify's FusionAuth backing than from openarchiver's undocumented ownership.

rows.note: Permify Community is AGPL-3.0 and free
Permify Cloud
Permify: Community free / Cloud custom

faqs.question: Are openarchiver and Permify competitors?
faqs.answer: No. openarchiver archives email for compliance and eDiscovery, while Permify is an authorization engine for controlling access inside applications. They address different problems, though both are open-source and self-hostable.

faqs.question: Does openarchiver connect to Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace?
faqs.answer: Yes, along with generic IMAP servers and PST/EML file imports for historical archives without a live mailbox connection.

faqs.question: Is Permify free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Permify's core Community edition is open source under AGPL-3.0 and can be self-hosted for free. A managed Permify Cloud option is also available with custom, usage-based pricing.

faqs.question: Does openarchiver publish its pricing?
faqs.answer: No specific pricing plans are documented for openarchiver, even though it's tagged as freemium. Permify, by contrast, documents a free Community tier and a custom-priced Cloud tier.

faqs.question: Can openarchiver and Permify be used together?
faqs.answer: Potentially — an organization could use Permify to govern who can access which archived mailboxes or search results inside a custom compliance tool, while openarchiver handles the underlying email archiving and full-text search.

faqs.question: Which tool offers a managed cloud version?
faqs.answer: Permify offers Permify Cloud, a managed hosting option with SOC2 and GDPR compliance across AWS, GCP, and Azure. openarchiver does not document a managed cloud version.

content.verdict: FleetDM is an open-source device management platform built on osquery for managing macOS, Linux, Windows, iOS, and Android devices from one console. openarchiver is an open-source, self-hosted platform for email backup, compliance, and eDiscovery with full-text search. Both are open-source IT/security infrastructure tools that can be self-hosted, but they manage completely different assets — FleetDM manages endpoint devices, openarchiver manages archived email.
content.bestForToolA: FleetDM fits IT and security teams needing cross-platform device inventory, patch and vulnerability management, and live queries across macOS, Linux, Windows, iOS, and Android, with data reported back in under 30 seconds and a free open-source tier the company states will always remain free.
content.bestForToolB: openarchiver fits IT and compliance teams needing to archive email from Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, or IMAP, or import historical PST/EML files, for backup, eDiscovery, and full-text search.
content.whoNeedsBoth: An IT department could run FleetDM to manage and patch its fleet of employee devices while separately running openarchiver to archive company email for compliance and legal discovery — two distinct but complementary parts of an organization's IT and compliance stack.

keyDifferences.title: Core Purpose
keyDifferences.toolA: FleetDM manages and monitors physical and virtual endpoint devices — inventory, patches, vulnerabilities, and live diagnostics — built on the osquery agent.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver archives email communications for backup, compliance, and eDiscovery, with full-text search across archived messages.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Device management and email archiving are distinct IT disciplines with different data models, so the right tool depends entirely on what asset needs managing.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: IT teams managing hardware/software fleets need FleetDM; compliance and legal teams handling email retention need openarchiver.

keyDifferences.title: Deployment Options
keyDifferences.toolA: FleetDM can be run as a self-hosted open-source deployment or as a hosted cloud service.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver is documented as self-hosted, with no managed cloud version documented.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Having both self-hosted and managed cloud options gives teams flexibility to start without infrastructure and migrate later if needed.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams wanting deployment flexibility benefit from FleetDM's dual options; openarchiver users must currently plan for self-hosting.

keyDifferences.title: Pricing Transparency
keyDifferences.toolA: FleetDM's Free, open-source tier is stated to "always remain free," with a custom-priced Premium tier for additional management and automation features.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver is tagged as freemium, but no specific pricing plans are documented.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: An explicit commitment to a permanently free tier gives buyers more confidence in long-term cost planning than an undocumented freemium model.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Budget-planning IT teams get clearer signal from FleetDM's documented free-tier commitment.

keyDifferences.title: Scale and Adoption Evidence
keyDifferences.toolA: FleetDM states it is used by 1,300+ organizations across 90+ countries, managing over 2,000,000 devices.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver does not publish comparable adoption or scale figures in the available facts.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Publicly stated adoption numbers can serve as a proof point for enterprise buyers evaluating vendor maturity.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Enterprises wanting evidence of at-scale usage get more documented signal from FleetDM.

keyDifferences.title: Automation and API
keyDifferences.toolA: FleetDM device configuration and policies can be managed as code and automated via a single REST API, with live queries and scripting supported against endpoints.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver does not document a REST API or infrastructure-as-code workflow in the available facts.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: API-driven, infrastructure-as-code management matters for teams that want to automate device policy changes at scale rather than click through a UI.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: DevOps-oriented IT teams benefit specifically from FleetDM's documented API and infrastructure-as-code support.

rows.feature: Free tier explicitly stated to remain free

rows.note: FleetDM: Free tier documented, Premium is custom-priced

faqs.question: Are FleetDM and openarchiver competitors?
faqs.answer: No. FleetDM manages endpoint devices (inventory, patching, vulnerabilities), while openarchiver archives email for compliance and eDiscovery. They cover different parts of an IT and compliance stack.

faqs.question: Is FleetDM free to use?
faqs.answer: Fleet maintains a free, open-source version that the company states will always be free, alongside a custom-priced Premium tier with additional management and automation features.

faqs.question: Does openarchiver publish its pricing?
faqs.answer: No specific pricing plans are documented for openarchiver, even though it's tagged as freemium.

faqs.question: Can either tool be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, both can. FleetDM supports self-hosted or hosted cloud deployment, while openarchiver is documented as self-hosted with no managed cloud option listed.

faqs.question: Which tool has a documented REST API?
faqs.answer: FleetDM documents a single REST API supporting infrastructure-as-code device management and automation. openarchiver does not document a comparable API in the available facts.

faqs.question: Would an organization need both tools?
faqs.answer: Many IT departments would run both for different purposes — FleetDM to manage and secure employee devices, and openarchiver to archive email communications for legal and compliance retention requirements.

content.verdict: openarchiver is an open-source, self-hosted platform for email backup, compliance, and eDiscovery with full-text search. Velociraptor is a free, open-source digital forensics and incident response (DFIR) tool for endpoint monitoring and threat hunting, using its own Velociraptor Query Language (VQL). Both are open-source, self-hosted security and compliance infrastructure, but openarchiver preserves email records while Velociraptor investigates and hunts across live endpoints.
content.bestForToolA: openarchiver fits IT and compliance teams that need to archive email from Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, or IMAP, or import historical PST/EML files, for backup, eDiscovery, and full-text search.
content.bestForToolB: Velociraptor fits security and incident response teams that need to write custom VQL queries for targeted forensic evidence collection, continuous endpoint monitoring, and threat hunting across large, distributed fleets, maintained by Rapid7 since its 2021 acquisition of the Velociraptor team.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A security team investigating a suspected breach could use Velociraptor to hunt for malicious activity and collect forensic evidence across endpoints, while pulling relevant archived email from openarchiver as part of the same eDiscovery or incident timeline.

keyDifferences.title: Core Purpose
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver archives email communications for backup, compliance, and eDiscovery, with full-text search across archived messages.
keyDifferences.toolB: Velociraptor collects forensic evidence and hunts for threats across endpoints, monitoring logs, file changes, and processes using its own VQL query language.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Email archiving and endpoint forensics address different stages and data types within security and compliance work.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Compliance/legal teams handling email retention need openarchiver; incident responders investigating endpoint activity need Velociraptor.

keyDifferences.title: Data Focus
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver focuses on email data from Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, IMAP, and PST/EML file imports.
keyDifferences.toolB: Velociraptor focuses on endpoint telemetry — logs, file changes, and processes — across potentially tens of thousands of machines in real incident response operations.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The type of data source directly determines which tool is relevant to a given investigation or compliance requirement.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams responding to endpoint-based incidents need Velociraptor's telemetry focus; teams managing email retention need openarchiver's mailbox focus.

keyDifferences.title: Licensing and Backing
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver's short description calls it the "leading open-source email archiving solution," but no specific license or company backing is documented.
keyDifferences.toolB: Velociraptor is explicitly licensed under AGPL and maintained by Rapid7, which acquired the Velociraptor team in 2021.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A named license and a documented maintaining company give buyers clearer signal about legal terms and long-term support.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Risk-averse buyers evaluating open-source infrastructure get more documented certainty from Velociraptor's explicit AGPL license and Rapid7 backing.

keyDifferences.title: Deployment Model
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver is documented as self-hosted, with no managed cloud version and no vendor-run SaaS documented.
keyDifferences.toolB: Velociraptor also requires self-hosting and operational management, with no vendor-run SaaS version of the open-source tool itself — Rapid7 separately sells a commercial InsightIDR/XDR product that integrates Velociraptor.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Neither tool offers a fully managed SaaS version of its core open-source product, so both require the buyer to run and operate the infrastructure.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Technical teams comfortable with self-hosted operations are the fit for both tools; teams wanting a fully managed SaaS product would need to look at Rapid7's separate commercial offerings for forensics.

keyDifferences.title: Query and Customization Model
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver does not document a custom query language, relying instead on full-text search over archived email.
keyDifferences.toolB: Velociraptor's VQL lets investigators write fully custom forensic collections and threat hunts, backed by a built-in library of forensic artifacts, though it has a documented learning curve.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A custom query language enables highly specific, repeatable investigations that a fixed search interface can't replicate.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Forensic analysts building tailored, repeatable hunts benefit specifically from Velociraptor's VQL.

faqs.question: Are openarchiver and Velociraptor competitors?
faqs.answer: No. openarchiver archives email for compliance and eDiscovery, while Velociraptor collects forensic evidence and hunts for threats across live endpoints. They address different stages of security and compliance work.

faqs.question: Is Velociraptor free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Velociraptor is open source under the AGPL license and free to use, maintained by Rapid7 following its 2021 acquisition of the Velociraptor team.

faqs.question: Does openarchiver publish its pricing?
faqs.answer: No specific pricing plans are documented for openarchiver, even though it's tagged as freemium.

faqs.question: What is VQL?
faqs.answer: VQL (Velociraptor Query Language) is Velociraptor's query language for writing custom forensic collections and threat-hunting queries, backed by a built-in library of forensic artifacts.

faqs.question: Can openarchiver and Velociraptor be used together?
faqs.answer: Yes — during a security investigation, a team could use Velociraptor to hunt for malicious activity and collect endpoint evidence while pulling relevant archived email from openarchiver as part of the same eDiscovery timeline.

faqs.question: Does either tool offer a fully managed SaaS version?
faqs.answer: No. Neither openarchiver nor Velociraptor's open-source tool documents a vendor-run SaaS version — both require self-hosting, though Rapid7 separately sells a commercial InsightIDR/XDR product that integrates Velociraptor.
